content:
I need to use the logic of a custom format specifier (%fwf) in Rust. The logic for it is defined in a static function fwnode_full_name_string.

Has this been done before? I suppose it's not OK to make this function non-static. But reimplementing the logic in Rust also doesn't seem ideal.

I suppose it's not OK to make this function non-static.

Is there a reason for that? i.e. if that is the best way to proceed, it may be OK -- you will probably want to check with the maintainers.

I just assumed so. Exporting stuff for use with Rust makes it accessible to other C code too, right? Like drivers written in C. I would understand if maintainers are opposed to encapsulation being torn down because of Rust. But I will verify that exporting the function works for me and then ask the maintainers if it's OK.

Yeah, they may or may not want to do it, but if you think it would be a good approach, then I would suggest asking, i.e. avoiding to assume they wouldn't want to change it at all, if that makes sense.

As for other parts of C being able to access it -- that is correct, but it wouldn't be the first API at all out there that is generally not meant to be used except in particular/specialized cases.

It could also be mitigated by e.g. not providing it in the usual header (or any header, actually, if they are really concerned about it, though you probably want at least one to pass it to bindgen) and adding a couple __ as a prefix.
